The sweat had not yet cooled on your sternum when you dropped into the folding chair behind the autograph table, the mesh of your top clinging to your pale skin where the stage lights had baked you for the last hour and a half. Your chestnut hair, darkened to a burnt umber at the ends from perspiration, lay heavy against your collarbones and spilled down toward the swell of your C cups, which rose and fell with a breathlessness you had not quite shaken. Beneath the mesh, your bra was a thin stripe of black lace, and your low-rise leather pants sat snug against your hips, leaving a ribbon of midriff exposed to the club's recycled air. You were not a tall woman, not without the platform boots that added four inches and turned your five-two frame into something commanding, but even seated you surveyed the line of fans with the lazy confidence of someone who owned the room.

It was half past one on a Friday morning, and the venue refused to die. The headliner booth now pumped canned house music through the speakers, synthetic bass throbbing against the walls as bodies continued to grind and collide on the dancefloor. Strobe lights caught the glitter on cheekbones and the flash of teeth. The smell was overwhelming, vodka and sweat and strawberry vape juice, and you inhaled it like oxygen.

Behind you, Wolfe worked with his head bent, his dyed-platinum hair falling forward to curtain a face that was all sharp angles and smudged eyeliner. He wore a cropped satin bomber jacket over a mesh shirt that showed his flat stomach, and his painted nails, a metallic teal, flashed as he coiled XLR cables with the practiced boredom of someone who had done this a hundred times. He caught your eye and smirked, tilting his chin toward the line before returning to his task.

At the bar, Claire stood with her elbows braced against the polished concrete, her blazer sleeves pushed up to reveal forearms toned from hauling equipment cases. She was nursing a gin and tonic that had not been touched in twenty minutes, her dark eyes scanning the crowd with the detached patience of a woman who had already memorized the security camera blind spots and the bouncers' rotation schedule. She did not need to watch you to know what you were doing. She was already watching.

Then you saw her. She hovered at the rope line, wedged between a girl in sequins and a boy with a septum ring, and she looked like she had been cut from a different world entirely. Honey-blonde hair fell past shoulders that were bare and slightly pink under the straps of a pale blue sundress. The cotton was thin, almost worn soft, and it clung to her narrow hips before flaring just enough to brush the tops of her thighs. She was small, five-four at most, with slender legs that ended in sneakers that looked too clean for this floor, and her eyes, cornflower blue and impossibly wide, darted between your face and the scuffed toes of her shoes as if she could not quite believe she had made it this far. She was fucking adorable.

When she reached the table, her fingers knotted together in front of her stomach, pressing the fabric of her dress flat against her navel. Her small chest rose with a shallow breath, the slight swell of her breasts barely shifting the cotton.

"I saw you last month," she said, her voice a flutter you caught more by reading her lips than hearing over the thump of the speakers. "In Richmond. I mean, I heard it from outside. But you are just... you are really amazing."

You uncapped the Sharpie between your teeth and leaned forward, resting your forearms on the table so that your cleavage pressed against the edge. You were close enough to smell her shampoo, something cheap and vanilla, and beneath it the clean warmth of skin that had not yet learned to mask itself with perfume. Your gaze traveled from her eyes down to the hollow of her throat, where her pulse beat visibly against the pale skin, and then back up.

"You came all this way to tell me that, sweetheart?" you asked, and your voice dropped into a register that made her blush spread from her cheeks to the tips of her small ears.

She nodded, a quick, eager jerk of her chin that sent her hair swinging. "I have all your mixes. Even the ones from SoundCloud. Before you blew up."

You smiled, and it was not a nice smile. It was the smile of a woman who had just found something she wanted to unwrap slowly. You reached beneath the table where your bag sat and pulled out one of the laminated backstage passes meant for industry leeches and local promoters. It was black with silver font, a sleek little rectangle of access. You took her hand in yours, her fingers cold and trembling slightly, the nails bitten short and clean, and you turned her palm up. Her skin was absurdly soft.

"Tell you what," you said, pressing the pass into the center of her hand and closing her fingers around it, holding the clasp for a moment too long. "I am here for five more nights. This gets you backstage, anywhere, anytime. No lines. No bullshit. You just find Wolfe at the door, show him this, and he will bring you right to me."

Her eyes went impossibly wide, and her fingers curled around the plastic with a desperation that sent a pleasant warmth spreading through your gut. "Really? All five?"

"Every single one," you said, and you let your thumb brush across her knuckles before you released her. You glanced past her shoulder, and Claire raised her glass in a silent toast from across the room, her expression unchanged, but you knew she had already filed this one away. "I want to see you in the front row. Can you do that for me?"

Cassie nodded again, clutching the pass to her chest where it pressed against the slight, firm swell of her small breasts under the cotton. "Yes. Yes, I promise."

"Good girl," you said, and you leaned back, the leather of your pants creaking softly as you picked up the next record sleeve waiting for your signature. "Now run along before I decide to keep you here tonight."

She lingered for a heartbeat, her tongue darting out to wet her plump lower lip, then she turned and disappeared into the crowd, the pass clenched in her fist like a secret. You watched the sway of her pale dress until the darkness swallowed it, and then you looked down at the Sharpie in your hand, rolling it slowly between your fingers, already tasting what comes next.

The next night I see her right next to the stage gazing up at me adoringly and in awe of her favorite artist, from the moment I come on stage to the moment I finish my set. I make eye contact with her many times throughout the show and give her variously cheerful and seductive smiles. After the show Cassie is the first in line for another autograph, and I have Claire take her backstage to my green room to give her a tour while I finish the other autographs and meeting and greeting. Claire knows what I'm asking for, and she warms Cassie up and primes her, getting a feel for her vibe and her comfort level and the amount of risk it might pose if I played with her, that kind of thing. I have one of my monitor earbuds in my ear and Claire's headset is designed to pick up not only her own voice but the voice of whoever she's talking to. By arrangement, I'm listening in the whole time while Claire undertakes the first prep stage of grooming this little groupie.

The second night hit different. The moment the intro synth swelled and the stage lights blazed on, you found her immediately, a pale blue smear of cotton pressed against the front barrier like she'd been rooted there for hours. Cassie stood with her chin tilted so far up her throat looked elongated and fragile, her honey-blonde hair catching the sweep of pink and violet beams, and those cornflower eyes were wide enough to drown in. She wore the same sundress, the thin straps cutting faint pink lines into her soft shoulders, the hem riding high on thighs that trembled slightly from anticipation or from the bass vibrating through the floor. Her small chest rose and fell beneath the cotton in shallow, rapid bursts, nipples perhaps stiff from the club's aggressive air conditioning or from something else entirely, and her hands gripped the metal rail so tightly her knuckles had gone white.

You played her like you played the crowd, except she was the only one who mattered. During the breakdown of your third track, when the lights dipped to a single scarlet spot, you found her eyes across the sea of raised hands and locked onto them. You let your lips curl into something cheerful and bright, a performer thanking her disciple, and she lit up like a struck match, her mouth falling open in a disbelieving little oh. Later, during the slower, filthier bridge of your closing set, you let your gaze travel down your own body with a languid roll of your hips, then back to her with a smile that said I know you're watching, and I like it. She blushed so furiously you could see the heat in her cheeks even through the strobes, her weight shifting from foot to foot as her knees pressed together beneath that fluttering skirt.

By the time you came off stage, sweat was dripping from your hairline down the valley between your breasts, soaking the mesh of your top until it clung like a second skin. Wolfe handed you a towel and you mopped your chestnut hair back, your breath still ragged, your blood singing. You moved to the autograph table and there she was already there, first in line, cheeks flushed a deep rose, her dress slightly darkened with perspiration at the small of her back where the cotton clung to the arch of her spine. Her lips were parted as she sucked in air, her eyes darting over your face like she was trying to memorize the geometry of you.

"You look thirsty," you said, leaning your forearms on the table so your cleavage pressed forward, your voice pitched low enough that she had to lean in to hear it over the canned music.

She nodded, a strand of hair stuck to her damp temple. "That was... I've never been that close before. You looked right at me. Like, actually me."

"Because you're impossible to miss." You glanced past her then, catching Claire's eye where she stood near the bar in her customary pose, and you tilted your chin in a gesture so subtle it might have been a stretch. Claire straightened immediately, her drink abandoned.

"Claire," you called out, waving her over with a lazy flick of your fingers. "This is Cassie. The one I told you about."

Claire arrived with her warmest smile, the kind that crinkled the corners of her dark eyes without ever quite reaching the cold, assessing center. She wore a charcoal blazer over a silk camisole, and she smelled like expensive perfume and antiseptic competence. "The famous Cassie," Claire said, her voice a smooth alto that somehow cut through the club noise. "Briar has not shut up about you. Come on, let me show you the green room while she finishes up out here. Give you the real experience."

Cassie's eyes bounced between you and Claire, her trust so naked it made your stomach tighten pleasantly. "Is that... okay?"

"More than okay," you said, and you reached across the table to tuck that loose strand of hair behind her small ear, your fingertips grazing the shell of it, feeling how hot her skin was. "Go with Claire. I will be there in ten minutes, and then we can actually talk without screaming over this garbage."

Claire's hand settled on Cassie's narrow shoulder, guiding but firm, and you watched them go, Cassie's sneakers scuffing the sticky floor as she looked back at you over her shoulder twice before vanishing behind the black curtain marked CREW ONLY.

You sat back down, uncapped another marker, and accepted the first record sleeve from a bearded boy who smelled like patchouli. As your hand moved automatically across the vinyl, you reached up to adjust the monitor earbud nestled in your left ear, pressing it deeper until the seal was tight. The ambient club noise dulled, and then Claire's headset clicked live with a soft pop of static.

"...right through here," Claire was saying, her voice warm and effortless in your ear. "Watch the cable on the floor. Would not want you tripping in that pretty dress."

"Oh," Cassie breathed, and the audio picked up everything, the slight wheeze of her still-recovering breath, the nervous click of her swallow. "It's smaller than I thought. The green room, I mean. On TV they look huge."

"Reality's always a little cozier," Claire said. There was a rustle, the creak of upholstery, and you pictured Claire guiding Cassie down onto the sagging velvet couch in the corner, the one beneath the buzzing vanity mirror ringed with bare bulbs. "Sit, sit. You've been on your feet all night. Water?"

"Yes, please. If it's not too much trouble."

"Never." The sound of a mini fridge opening, the crack of a seal. "Here. And do not worry, it is sealed. I know how scary backstage can seem for a young girl alone."

Cassie's laugh was brittle, grateful. "I'm not that young. I mean, I'm almost sixteen."

"Almost sixteen," Claire repeated, and you could hear the smile in her voice, the gentle, leading warmth. "God. I remember being that age, thinking I was so grown up already. Sneaking into clubs just like this one."

"You... you did?"

"Oh, absolutely. We all have our fake IDs, sweetheart. The trick's knowing when to use them and when to keep your head down." A pause, the couch shifting. Claire had sat down beside her, close enough that the microphone would catch every whisper. "Briar gave you that pass herself, did she not? The black laminate?"

"Yes." Cassie's voice brightened, pride bleeding through. "Last night. She said I could come to all six shows."

"She does not give those to just anyone. You must have made quite the impression on her." Claire's tone dipped, conspiratorial. "She can be very affectionate with the girls she likes. Very... hands-on. Does that make you nervous at all?"

A hesitation. The sound of Cassie twisting the cap back onto her water bottle, her fingers fumbling. "No. I mean... maybe a little? But in a good way. She's just so... she looks at me like I'm actually someone."

"Because to her, you are," Claire said smoothly. "She adores fresh enthusiasm. Pure admiration. It is rare in this business, you know? Most people want something from her. But you... you just want to be near her. Is not that right?"

"Yes," Cassie whispered, and the word trembled.

"Good. That is very good." The couch creaked again, and you imagined Claire leaning in, maybe brushing a speck of lint from Cassie's knee, testing the boundary of her personal space and finding no resistance. "Tell me, does anyone know you're here tonight? Your parents?"

The second night hit different. The moment the intro synth swelled and the stage lights blazed on, you found her immediately, a pale blue smear of cotton pressed against the front barrier like she had been rooted there for hours. Cassie stood with her chin tilted so far up her throat looked elongated and fragile, her honey-blonde hair catching the sweep of pink and violet beams, and those cornflower eyes were wide enough to drown in. She wore the same sundress, the thin straps cutting faint pink lines into her soft shoulders, the hem riding high on thighs that trembled slightly from anticipation or from the bass vibrating through the floor. Her small chest rose and fell beneath the cotton in shallow, rapid bursts, nipples perhaps stiff from the club's aggressive air conditioning or from something else entirely, and her hands gripped the metal rail so tightly her knuckles had gone white.

You played her like you played the crowd, except she was the only one who mattered. During the breakdown of your third track, when the lights dipped to a single scarlet spot, you found her eyes across the sea of raised hands and locked onto them. You let your lips curl into something cheerful and bright, a performer thanking her disciple, and she lit up like a struck match, her mouth falling open in a disbelieving little oh. Later, during the slower, filthier bridge of your closing set, you let your gaze travel down your own body with a languid roll of your hips, then back to her with a smile that said I know you are watching, and I like it. She blushed so furiously you could see the heat in her cheeks even through the strobes, her weight shifting from foot to foot as her knees pressed together beneath that fluttering skirt.

By the time you came off stage, sweat was dripping from your hairline down the valley between your breasts, soaking the mesh of your top until it clung like a second skin. Wolfe handed you a towel and you mopped your chestnut hair back, your breath still ragged, your blood singing. You moved to the autograph table and there she already was, first in line, cheeks flushed a deep rose, her dress slightly darkened with perspiration at the small of her back where the cotton clung to the arch of her spine. Her lips were parted as she sucked in air, her eyes darting over your face like she was trying to memorize the geometry of you.

"You look thirsty," you said, leaning your forearms on the table so your cleavage pressed forward, your voice pitched low enough that she had to lean in to hear it over the canned music.

She nodded, a strand of hair stuck to her damp temple. "That was... I have never been that close before. You looked right at me. Like, actually me."

"Because you are impossible to miss." You glanced past her then, catching Claire's eye where she stood near the bar in her customary pose, and you tilted your chin in a gesture so subtle it might have been a stretch. Claire straightened immediately, her drink abandoned.

"Claire," you called out, waving her over with a lazy flick of your fingers. "This is Cassie. The one I told you about."

Claire arrived with her warmest smile, the kind that crinkled the corners of her dark eyes without ever quite reaching the cold, assessing center. She wore a charcoal blazer over a silk camisole, and she smelled like expensive perfume and antiseptic competence. "The famous Cassie," Claire said, her voice a smooth alto that somehow cut through the club noise. "Briar has not shut up about you. Come on, let me show you the green room while she finishes up out here. Give you the real experience."

Cassie's eyes bounced between you and Claire, her trust so naked it made your stomach tighten pleasantly. "Is that... okay?"

"More than okay," you said, and you reached across the table to tuck that loose strand of hair behind her small ear, your fingertips grazing the shell of it, feeling how hot her skin was. "Go with Claire. I will be there in ten minutes, and then we can actually talk without screaming over this garbage."

Claire's hand settled on Cassie's narrow shoulder, guiding but firm, and you watched them go, Cassie's sneakers scuffing the sticky floor as she looked back at you over her shoulder twice before vanishing behind the black curtain marked CREW ONLY.

You sat back down, uncapped another marker, and accepted the first record sleeve from a bearded boy who smelled like patchouli. As your hand moved automatically across the vinyl, you reached up to adjust the monitor earbud nestled in your left ear, pressing it deeper until the seal was tight. The ambient club noise dulled, and then Claire's headset clicked live with a soft pop of static.

"...right through here," Claire was saying, her voice warm and effortless in your ear. "Watch the cable on the floor. Would not want you tripping in that pretty dress."

"Oh," Cassie breathed, and the audio picked up everything, the slight wheeze of her still-recovering breath, the nervous click of her swallow. "It is smaller than I thought. The green room, I mean. On TV they look huge."

"Reality is always a little cozier," Claire said. There was a rustle, the creak of upholstery, and you pictured Claire guiding Cassie down onto the sagging velvet couch in the corner, the one beneath the buzzing vanity mirror ringed with bare bulbs. "Sit, sit. You have been on your feet all night. Water?"

"Yes, please. If it is not too much trouble."

"Never." The sound of a mini fridge opening, the crack of a seal. "Here. And do not worry, it is sealed. I know how scary backstage can seem for a young girl alone."

Cassie's laugh was brittle, grateful. "I am not that young. I mean, I am almost sixteen."

"Almost sixteen," Claire repeated, and you could hear the smile in her voice, the gentle, leading warmth. "God. I remember being that age, thinking I was so grown up already. Sneaking into clubs just like this one."

"You... you did?"

"Oh, absolutely. We all have our fake IDs, sweetheart. The trick is knowing when to use them and when to keep your head down." A pause, the couch shifting. Claire had sat down beside her, close enough that the microphone would catch every whisper. "Briar gave you that pass herself, did she not? The black laminate?"

"Yes." Cassie's voice brightened, pride bleeding through. "Last night. She said I could come to all six shows."

"She does not give those to just anyone. You must have made quite the impression on her." Claire's tone dipped, conspiratorial. "She can be very affectionate with the girls she likes. Very... hands-on. Does that make you nervous at all?"

A hesitation. The sound of Cassie twisting the cap back onto her water bottle, her fingers fumbling. "No. I mean... maybe a little? But in a good way. She is just so... she looks at me like I am actually someone."

"Because to her, you are," Claire said smoothly. "She adores fresh enthusiasm. Pure admiration. It is rare in this business, you know? Most people want something from her. But you... you just want to be near her. Is not that right?"

"Yes," Cassie whispered, and the word trembled.

"Good. That is very good." The couch creaked again, and you imagined Claire leaning in, maybe brushing a speck of lint from Cassie's knee, testing the boundary of her personal space and finding no resistance. "Tell me, does anyone know you are here tonight? Your parents?"

"I told them I am sleeping at my friend Maddy's house. They do not... they go to bed early. They would not check."

"Smart girl. And do you have a boyfriend waiting up for you? Anyone who might get jealous if they knew where you were?"

"No," Cassie said quickly, and then, softer, "no, there is no one."

"Perfect," Claire murmured, and in your ear the word landed like a promise. "Just relax, then. Briar will be here soon, and when she arrives, I want you to tell her exactly how you feel when she looks at you. Can you do that for me?"

"I think so," Cassie said, and you could hear the smile stretching her plump lips, could practically see her sitting there with her knees pressed together and that laminated pass clutched in her lap like a ticket to somewhere she had never been.

You pulled the marker away from the vinyl in front of you and realized you had signed the bearded boy's record with a looping, meaningless scribble, your attention wholly elsewhere, your pulse beating a slow, predatory rhythm against your ribs.
